---
title: "Teaching"
subtitle: "Designing the next generation of organizations — platforms, ecosystems, and AI-embedded firms."
---

## Positioning

I teach the economics and strategy of designing the next generation of organizations — platforms, ecosystems, and AI-embedded firms — built from first principles and grounded in frontier research. The through-line across my MBA, honors, undergraduate, and PhD courses is the same: take the most current research in economics, technology, management, and innovation, and turn it into frameworks you can actually use to design a business, not just describe one. Most courses in this space either teach AI without the underlying economics, or teach economics without the building. Mine does both, as one coherent argument.

## What I teach — the argument

My courses are built around a single argument in four stages:

1. **Value creation.** Where does value come from in a digital, networked economy? We start with users and use cases, network effects, and the dimensions of value — and derive value curves that generalize Porter for platforms and ecosystems.
2. **Value delivery.** How do you build the operating model? Features and functions, how to organize the sides of a platform (markets, contests, communities, networks), open vs. closed choices, ecosystem orchestration, launch and tipping.
3. **Value capture.** How do you sustain advantage? Pricing, scale economies, portfolios of advantage, organization-specific assets, freemium. Network effects treated as *designed*, not inherent.
4. **AI and the prediction factory.** How machine intelligence gets embedded in the operating model: task selection, data inventory, algorithm design, integration, and the move to generative AI, agents, and LLMs.

Each module builds on the last. The frameworks are robust enough to outlast any particular technology cycle because they sit on how value is created, delivered, and captured — questions that do not change even as the tools do.

## Courses

**MBA Strategy (D'Amore-McKim, Northeastern).** Core strategy for MBAs. Traditional strategy tools (Five Forces, VRIO, positioning) extended and, where necessary, replaced for ecosystems, platforms, and AI-native firms.

**Honors Platforms (HONR 3310).** Year-long honors course. Students move through the full four-module arc and deliver original research presentations in the spring.

**INNO 3303 — Platform Business Models.** Undergraduate course. Same intellectual architecture, pitched at the undergraduate level. Students design platforms and build AI prototypes.

**PhD seminar.** Research training in the economics of innovation, platforms, and digital organization. Paper-by-paper, emphasis on research design and method-to-question fit.

**Executive and guest teaching.** Short, sharp sessions for executive programs, conferences, and corporate audiences. Same frameworks, compressed.

## Signature topics and frameworks

A selection of the most distinctive things I teach. Each is a unit of class time, not a buzzword.

- **User × Use Case and the Dimensions of Value.** A design tool for locating where platform value actually sits — before you pick a business model.
- **Value curves beyond Porter.** An extension of Porter's positioning logic for multi-sided and stack-based businesses where "the industry" is the wrong unit of analysis.
- **Four models for organizing the sides of a platform.** Markets, contests, communities, networks — when to use each, and why the choice locks in everything that follows.
- **Open vs. closed as a make-or-buy decision.** Platform boundary choice as an organizational-economics problem, not a religion.
- **Tipping and winner-take-all — under what conditions.** The three conditions that must be met for WTA. Usually they aren't. Saying so out loud changes strategy.
- **The prediction-factory pipeline.** Task selection → data inventory → algorithm design → integration. A repeatable sequence for building AI into the operating model rather than bolting it on.
- **Strong AI, LLMs, and agents in the operating model.** What changes when prediction gets general, cheap, and agentic — and what doesn't.
- **Platform Battle Royale.** A multi-round competitive simulation students run in class. Pricing, complementor management, network effects, and defection dynamics in real time.

## What students do

My courses are design-oriented, not descriptive. In a given term, students will:

- Discuss 15+ real cases (Amazon, Uber, Wikipedia, Quantopian, eHarmony, Peloton, Upwork, Jibo, Ripple, Anthropic, Hugging Face, and more).
- Design an original platform: blueprint, operating model, pricing, launch plan.
- Build an AI prototype using current tools (Anthropic agents, LLMs) tied to the operating model they designed.
- Run the Platform Battle Royale simulation against their classmates.
- Read and evaluate academic papers — in the honors and PhD courses, present original research or research proposals.
- Write short, argued memos (no filler) analyzing platform situations.

## Research → teaching

The frameworks I teach come directly out of research I'm doing now, not a textbook I wrote a decade ago. A few live examples:

- **Platform boundaries, freemium, and profiting from digital innovation** (Boudreau, Miric, Jeppesen; published work) → Modules 1–3 on value creation, delivery, and capture on multi-sided platforms.
- **Marginal complementors** (working paper) → the "unpaid complementors" and platform-governance sessions.
- **Digitization, AI, and search-and-matching in modern labor markets** (Boudreau, Jungbauer, Williams) → the labor-market and AI-in-operations discussions in Module 4.
- **Co-op timing and engineering careers** (Boudreau & Marx, in revision) → how human capital is formed under uncertainty; used in discussions of early-career design and talent architectures.
- **Multidisciplinary research experiment** (Boudreau & Ganguli) → who selects into novel, cross-disciplinary work; used in the innovation and crowds sessions and in the PhD seminar.
- **Motivations in open innovation** (Boudreau; Boudreau & Dudley meta-analysis) → the "contests, communities, and crowds" class sessions and the Quantopian case.
- **NBER chapter on experiments in scientific evaluation** → the research-methods training in the honors and PhD courses.
- **Industry analysis for ecosystems** (teaching note) → the replacement framework I use instead of a vanilla Five Forces lecture.

The principle: every module on the syllabus has at least one live research project feeding it. Students are not learning "what we knew in 2015" — they are learning what I'm working out this year.

## For different audiences

**Prospective students at Northeastern.** If you're thinking about taking a course with me, skim the four-module arc above. Honors and undergraduate sections take the full year; the MBA version is a compressed strategy core.

**Program directors and conference organizers.** I teach in executive programs and give keynotes on platform strategy, AI and the prediction factory, and experiments in innovation research. Reach out via email.

**Prospective PhD students.** My PhD teaching and advising focus on the economics of innovation, platforms, and the science of science. I prefer students with a clear methodological orientation and a willingness to design their own instruments.
